article.title: AMD, Arm, and Qualcomm Invest $60 Million in Wayve, Giving the Self-Driving Startup Backing From Every Major Automotive Chip Vendor
article.summary: Wayve's Series D extension brings total funding to $1.26 billion as the UK startup secures investment from four chipmakers whose silicon underpins virtually every automotive compute platform.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Chipmakers AMD, Arm, and Qualcomm Ventures have invested $60 million in UK autonomous driving startup Wayve, extending its Series D round to $1.26 billion and giving the company investment relationships with all four major providers of automotive compute silicon. The extension, announced April 15, follows NVIDIA's participation in the original $1.2 billion close in February 2026, as reported by [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/15/chipmakers-amd-arm-and-qualcomm-are-all-investing-in-this-buzzy-self-driving-tech-startup/).

The investment is as much a strategic signal as a financial one: Wayve's core proposition is that its AI Driver runs across any vehicle and any hardware configuration without location-specific engineering, and backing from all major chip vendors reinforces that hardware-agnostic positioning.

This follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-02/27-wayve-raises-15-billion-at-86-billion-valuation-as-bmw-and-mercedes-abandon-level-3-signaling-a-tectonic-shift-in-autonomous-driving) on Wayve's $1.5 billion raise at an $8.6 billion valuation, which coincided with BMW and Mercedes-Benz abandoning their in-house Level 3 autonomous driving programs.

## What We Know

Wayve's technology uses an end-to-end neural network that learns driving behavior directly from sensor data, without relying on hand-coded rules or high-definition maps. The system supports two product lines: an L2+ "hands off" assisted-driving mode requiring driver attentiveness, and an L3/L4 "eyes off" fully autonomous mode for robotaxis and consumer vehicles, according to [The Next Web](https://thenextweb.com/news/wayve-60m-series-d-autonomous-driving).

"For embodied AI to scale, automakers need design choice and supply chain flexibility," CEO and co-founder Alex Kendall said, adding that the company is "building an AI Driver that works across the full automotive compute ecosystem, from architectures already used in millions of vehicles today to the platforms powering the next generation," as reported by [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/15/chipmakers-amd-arm-and-qualcomm-are-all-investing-in-this-buzzy-self-driving-tech-startup/).

The startup's commercial pipeline is advancing on multiple fronts. Nissan has committed to integrating Wayve's AI Driver into its ProPILOT advanced driver-assistance system for mass production vehicles expected in fiscal year 2027, according to [The Next Web](https://thenextweb.com/news/wayve-60m-series-d-autonomous-driving). Nissan and Uber have also signed a memorandum of understanding for a Tokyo robotaxi pilot using Nissan LEAF vehicles built on NVIDIA's DRIVE Hyperion platform with Wayve's AI Driver, targeting late 2026, as reported by [The Next Web](https://thenextweb.com/news/wayve-60m-series-d-autonomous-driving). Mercedes-Benz and Stellantis are also customers planning future deployments.

Uber has separately pledged $300 million in milestone-based funding contingent on Wayve's London robotaxi deployment, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/15/chipmakers-amd-arm-and-qualcomm-are-all-investing-in-this-buzzy-self-driving-tech-startup/). A London trial with Uber is also planned, with the broader ambition of rolling out across more than 10 cities globally.

Wayve's total funding now stands at approximately $1.5 billion. The existing investor base includes Microsoft, NVIDIA, SoftBank Vision Fund 2, Eclipse Ventures, Balderton Capital, Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan, Baillie Gifford, the British Business Bank, and Schroders Capital, according to [The Next Web](https://thenextweb.com/news/wayve-60m-series-d-autonomous-driving).

## What We Don't Know

Wayve has not disclosed an updated valuation following the $60 million extension; the most recent figure of $8.6 billion dates from the February close. The company has not detailed which specific AMD, Arm, or Qualcomm platforms it will prioritize for initial deployment, nor has it published benchmarks comparing its AI Driver's performance across different silicon vendors.

The Tokyo and London robotaxi timelines remain subject to regulatory approval, and it is unclear how Wayve's mapless approach will perform under the dense and complex conditions of those cities compared to its existing testing environments. The company tested its AI Driver across more than 500 cities in a zero-shot demonstration in 2025, but production deployment at scale remains unproven.

## Analysis

The chip vendor investment pattern is strategically significant. By securing backing from AMD, Arm, Qualcomm, and NVIDIA, Wayve has positioned itself as a platform play rather than a hardware-specific solution. This contrasts with competitors like Tesla, which designs its own silicon, and Waymo, which has relied primarily on custom compute stacks. For automakers evaluating autonomous driving partners, Wayve's silicon-agnostic approach reduces the risk of vendor lock-in at the compute layer.

The structure of the investment also matters. At $60 million, the chip companies are making a measured bet rather than a transformative one, suggesting this is primarily about securing early integration partnerships rather than scaling capital. The real commercial test will come when Wayve's AI Driver ships in Nissan production vehicles in 2027 and whether the Uber robotaxi pilots in Tokyo and London can demonstrate reliable performance without the HD maps and hand-tuned geofences that dominate current deployments.
